item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's also factually incorrect. No country uses Imperial anymore, because the standards of the Weights and Measures Act of 1824 are no longer maintained by the UK. In a legal sense, there is no Imperial System any longer.

The US, by the way does not now, nor has ever used the Imperial System, since we had been an independent state for about fifty years by the time it even existed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is years old. You're blustering at something unlikely to change.

DO NOT USE IMPERIAL. WE USE THE METRIC SYSTEM! Only three countries use imperial, while the rest of the world (including me) uses metric.

This only makes you come off like a smug jerk. Leave your crit and move on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The goddess of anorexia… it's a thing what makes you crazy. That and the fact it is not interesting enough already lost my upvote; and there's what I consider the worst tone problem: "approximately 5 inches in diameter" DO NOT USE IMPERIAL. WE USE THE METRIC SYSTEM! Only three countries use imperial, while the rest of the world (including me) uses metric.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice take on a mental illness too people underestimate. A few nitpicks -

You've taken everything from me, cast me aside like a rag doll, looked at my at disdain.

That last sentence, is it supposed to read "looking at me with distain" or something similar?

However, two days before his scheduled experiment with SCP-1052 (which would have involved him spending time alone with the object while observed by third parties), Senior Researcher ████ was found murdered in his home.

Observed? Or unobserved?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Asst. Researcher ███████: Answer the question, goddammit!"

instadownvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted - just not that interesting and VERY confusing as to what is causing what here. Does the mirror cause anorexia or is anorexia just a side effect of the mental hazard the mirror causes? Too confusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have three major criticisms of this article. From least to greatest:

1. I do not understand the timeline of its containment history. It has been with the Foundation for "at least over a century." Disregarding the problems with that syntax, the article goes on to say "Current containment procedure has had been in effect since 1948 […] The connection between the object and the letters was made after SCP-1052 was transferred to Site 54 in the aftermath of Site 26's destruction, and the letters continued being sent, now instead posted to the location of Site 45." Earlier in the article there is the line: "The mirror itself displays no anomalous properties other than letter-receiving."

Why, then, was the object in the Foundations custody? If they didn't connect the letters to the object, why was it at site 26 in the first place? The article states that there's no anomalous properties other than the letter receiving. If they didn't connect the letters to the object, they thus wouldn't have thought it was anomalous, and if it wasn't anomalous, they wouldn't have contained it. This really needs to be explained with more detail.

2. Speaking of anomalous properties, its letter receiving is entirely non-anomalous. It stands to reason, to a certain extent, that people who receive letters will then send them back. It's letter sending that's anomalous. I would describe this literary device, where it's described as one thing and then later on revealed as another, as a bait-and-switch, though that's not a technical term. I do not think it is character for the Foundation to keep that bait-and-switch in its files, either. It's one thing to use an addendum to add additional information about an SCP as it is acquired; it's quite another to have an addendum that reveals that the initial assessment of the object was completely incorrect. Whoever is keeping the files on this object would instead rewrite the initial description.

Even worse is that the reality, that the object sends letters posted from its current location, with the logical result that it receives letters there as well, is only relayed in an interview log instead of described directly by any Foundation personnel, is incredibly sloppy. In any sort of real organization whoever was in charge of this one would be looking at a reprimand of some sort.

Unfortunately, outside of viewing this in the context of how the Foundation would actually write these files and looking at it as literature, the whole impact of the article is predicated on this bait-and-switch, which is unfortunate, because it really makes it hard for me to suspend disbelief.

3. Third, and most importantly, this object is totally un-contained. Sure, it is physically in a Foundation facility, but nothing they are doing in this article is actually containing its anomalous properties. They are not protecting anyone from them, and they're barely protecting the object from anyone else. Any one of these people could easily decide to track down this "Ana" who is sending them unsolicited mail, instead of writing back to her. One letter sent to some sort of reporter would definitely require amnestics or risk breach of secrecy. Considering that they've already had one site destroyed in a military strike because of this object, and that it regularly hurts people by encouraging and possibly causing their mental illness, and that it's a constant risk of exposure for the Foundation, and that it's directly caused one murder of Foundation staff, it's pretty crazy, by which I mean unbelievable, that they're kind of maybe thinking about upgrading it to Euclid, but not enough to bother to getting around to rigorous testing to figure out if they can maybe stop it from sending letters, like they do with 1020. I would describe the current containment procedures as lackadaisical, at best, which is unworthy of the Foundation.

Anyway, down-voted, until it gets a major reworking. I don't have any problem with the concept, but it needs a lot of work on the execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To the above two posters defending me, thanks, I do appreciate it. As for those who dislike this article for its writing, well, I understand. Reading this two years on, even I cringe at some of my mistakes.

Now, for those who dislike it for the concept or for me using the letter, again, I understand that this is a touchy topic, perhaps I understand it moreso than most.

See, thing is, when I wrote this I was actually suffering from anorexia, most of the symptoms and slight mania (discounting, of course, the supernatural stuff) stemmed from personal experience. I didn't admit it here because I didn't admit it to anyone, even at times to myself. In fact, if I remember correctly, this may have been the phase where I became bullemic. Thankfully I am no longer either bullemic nor anorexic. Anyway, I understood the gravity of the topic when I wrote this, and even if I were not suffering from the same condition at the time, I believe that does not take away my right to write about it, or about any other condition. A real - life letter though, understandably, crossed the line for some, it being the product of someone else's madness, rather than (to be melodramatic) my own.

Anyway, long story short, the last thing I want is people to up vote this simply because it's an article dealing with my own past sickness - sympathy votes or anything similar are definitely not what I want. Just treat this as an apologia and a nice little conclusion to this article, which I've really got to clean up when I have the time, or maybe expound upon.

As for whether or not I actually believed in Ana, well, I'd like to say no… but there are times where I remember. I remember staring into my bathroom mirror and hearing voices laced with criticism, with the sickly sweet promise of easy comfort. Or a little thought urging me on to puke just one more time into that porcelain bowl. Maybe there's an Ana in every sufferer. Or maybe, just maybe…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm surprised that this story has such a low ranking. One thing I would like is for the subject they interviewed to be given access to Ana. One thing that makes many SCP's interesting is the possibility that there is nothing there and the danger is coming from within.
Maybe he see's it and is confused that they dragged him to God knows where and locked him in a room just to look at a damn mirror.
Metric VS imperial measurement was brought up in the comments. I think some people make too big a deal about it but it is more likely that scientists would use metric.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I skimmed down this (I don't remember why) so that the first thing I read was the transcription of the "letter to Ana". I read it and thought, "How can an SCP with this letter in it have such a low rating?" After reading the entirety of the article, I still felt the same confusion regarding the low rating. Having now read the comments section, all I can do is facedesk.

Seriously? People downvoted because it was based on something real? We all get our inspirations from somewhere, no matter what it is we write! Building an SCP around a letter written by a person with Anorexia is no different than building an SCP around the random pictures people are encouraged to use to make new SCP's!

What the people here somehow seem to have missed is that it's a mirror that receives letters. That's pretty original as far as I can see - I mean, we like Dave the Lamp, and his quirk is solely that people treat him like a person (and then other light sources, yes, I know, but still). On top of that, though, this mirror also apparently sends letters as well. And did I mention that it's a mirror? Saying this is unoriginal because the author took a letter written by an actual sick person and wrote a story so that that letter was addressed to a mirror makes no sense at all.

And yes, it does imply that at least some cases of Anorexia are caused by this thing, but while I understand the stance that people doing that to themselves without any sort of outside influence is disturbing, 1) people with those disorders don't have control over it, they're being influenced by something they can't control (actually, if "Ana" causes Anorexia, then people are choosing to listen to an outside person, rather than responding to an uncontrollable reflex, so that point is moot), and 2) what makes this scarier than reality is the same thing that makes any SCP disturbing, and that is the fact that it cannot be explained by modern science - which is basically the foundation of the Foundation! Plus, don't any of you think it would be a lot scarier if there's an actual entity that causes Anorexia - that the afflicted people who write letters such as the one around which this SCP was constructed are contacting (and being contacted by) an actual conscious being, not just projecting their problems onto some imagined person?

And to go back to the point about downvoting because an actual letter written by a real person was the focal point for this article, that fact lends it realism, and isn't suspension of disbelief the lifeblood of this site? It's like the creatures that can only be seen by people with mental disorders - people with scary disorders that interfere with living a healthy life aren't imagining things in their minds, but rather, they're actually interacting with real entities that "normal" people simply can't perceive.

Quite honestly, if "Ana" was the cause of Anorexia, not whatever scientific explanation we have for that disorder in the real world, I'd be a lot more scared - this article squeed me out badly. This SCP is made up of the maybe-there monsters and Dave the Lamp, with a healthy dose of mysterious salesman letters/anomalous mail room and a good bit of the music sheet that makes you try to finish the piece even if it means you bleed to death (I can't remember their numbers or exact titles, but I bet old hands can guess what I'm talking about) - and those are all (pardon my language) damn good SCPs. If it's based on something real, that should make it rank above everything else, not below. Upvoted hard, and I'd upvote twenty times if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There WAS, from what I recall, a website that reflected the SCP's content linked to the article, or in the comments, or something. Several pages. Very convincing. I can't remember what ended up happening with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's this website the old posts here mention?

Do I want to know about it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't like it before, and I still don't like it now. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tried to downvote this, but the wiki reminded me that I already did, when it was first posted. Searching the thread, I'm in agreement with my pervious comments. This just feels like an exploitation piece, whatever the intentions, and the writing/SCP twist isn't interesting or intelligent enough to overcome the sour taste.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1052 is a ladies' hand-mirror, approximately 5 inches in diameter,

Located 2 inches from the head of the mirror,

diagnosed with Anorexia Nervosa

That should really be fixed.

Overall, it's not terrible and I feel like it tells a halfway decent story, but I don't care for it much, really. Downvoted.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Please take the time to read, rate and comment this article, it should be given more attention

I really wasn't hooked by this one. If there was a bit more meat to this article, I might like it more, but as it is…. I don't care for it enough to vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My main problem with this one is the glaring lack of metric.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: DICE FOR THE DICE THRONE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sigh. Even though this is well written, I've just seen this concept done before, and a bit too much. This version doesn't have enough new or interesting to make up for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I apologize for the glaring lack of test log. Will almost definitely upload a week from now, when Corerosion finally returns from the military. I want to run it by him first, seeing as we worked on this together.

i miss you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was withholding my upvote until the author got rid of the real letter. It was really creepy but it didn't sit right with me. The concept of an entity that feeds off the self-loathing of anorexics is great though. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe a bit about the Foundation recruiting anorexic D-class from various mental hospitals, if I can write a test log that doesn't make the article too long.

I like the way such a thing subtly implies that criminals aren't our only source of D-Class. Would make for a good addition if you two can pull it off. Of course, I upvoted already so not like it matters that much XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the Foundation would do at least one experiment to see what would happen if the SCP was shown to an anorexic who wrote to the SCP. Or at least shown photos of it.

Worth thinking about. Maybe a bit about the Foundation recruiting anorexic D-class from various mental hospitals, if I can write a test log that doesn't make the article too long.

Logically, the containment procedure should also include searches of anorexia-related forums for people reporting Ana writing back to them, but that would ruin the surprise of discovering that is does write back.

Hm. Ana writing back was Core's idea, and I'm not too clear on it, but seeing as he's not around to execute the rest of it, I'll see what I can do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the Foundation would do at least one experiment to see what would happen if the SCP was shown to an anorexic who wrote to the SCP. Or at least shown photos of it.

Security personnel are also to check the bi-weekly Internet word filter records for any instances where the term "Ana" is used in the same context as any of the Foundation buzzwords

Logically, the containment procedure should also include searches of anorexia-related forums for people reporting Ana writing back to them, but that would ruin the surprise of discovering that is does write back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea is suitably creepy. The procedure section could be abbreviated significantly without losing any impact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi, I'm Creek. I helped write this SCP.
About the original letter. I know that since it's been removed, this is kind of a non-issue, but I felt the need to clarify.
We could not credit the original author of this letter, for the reasons stated below.
This letter has attained a certain amount of fame in and about eating disorder circles, as a 'pledge' letter. As in, people are encouraged to copy/paste it, and add their name under 'Worthless One', in order to make public their commitment to their disease. This letter was probably created with that express purpose in mind, and as so, I felt more comfortable knowing that something akin to a meme was being used here, instead of some heartfelt letter from some poor girl, posting on her personal blog.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Enh. I'm not a fan of this idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Related.
What about an experiment log, perhaps in place of some of the longer text bits?
I wonder what'd happen if someone slapped a GPS receiver on one of the letters to Ana.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's actually a really interesting idea… I was thinking of it the other way around, that the GOC were using it to track the Foundation (although then again, the GOC hates the usage of any and all SCPs)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't see how it's any more tasteless than picture documentation for some other objects either (say, some of the pathogenic germs showing patients with actual sickness, or, as far as I know, the Old Man one showing a WWII napalm burned guy, (but I might be mistaken with that one))
That said, this wasn't a wonder object, and all those are better.
Also, no fuck would I argue for the removal of content from those. The point i'm trying to make is that it's odd how you get a very, very different reaction from some people simple due to change of medium of additional material used.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see how this was tasteless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvote for tasteless use of someone else's content removed, downvote for ridiculously overblown prose added.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if it fell into the GOC's hands 'by accident'? It could be used as a tracking device to keep tabs on them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-1337 - One of the reasons I like this object is that it shows how sometimes, attempts to destroy something can make the situation that much worse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And surely if it's effects are not being studied then they must have tried to destroy it at one point?

What if that made it worse, though? And who knows, it could be useful at some point in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, why hasn't there been more testing of it's anomalous properties? Like bury it in the desert and see if letters still arrive.

And surely if it's effects are not being studied then they must have tried to destroy it at one point?

item.title: Re: Staff post:
item.content:encoded:
Voting for a stay of deletion of… let's say, two days to give Corerosion some time to write a replacement letter; if no new content is made, consider this a reluctant second.

Wow, that was fast. I suppose it's a non-issue now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright. I think I can write a letter that sounds decently genuine, and I still can replace it. Thing is though, this stuff focused around me and my writing confuses me, and gets me down somewhat, in the sense that I've never thought of this site as a place where I can showcase whatever talent I have at writing. In fact, there are many places online where one can do that, but what the SCP-wiki means to me is that everyone is here in order to make the best site there is.

I'll be honest, I don't want to come across as blowing my own trumpet, but I feel that I could easily write a letter that is similar, but I also feel it wouldn't give as much of an impact. However, if everyone feels that originality of the contents is the more important point, then I am willing to change it.

Also, I'm not relying on the shock factor in order to make this SCP, there's no other motive behind me linking this and informing it to you all as a real site/thing other than to give credit for the letter to another person.

item.title: Staff post:
item.content:encoded: Advocating deletion for unoriginal content.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was suggesting something entirely original, not edited so much as modelled-after.

But yeah, link is certainly better than no link.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, it would bug me if the author didn't link in the original , for both reasons.
As of now ,not only he admits to not writing it, straight out, but linking the article to something external gives it a bit more fake credibility, which I like - such things might be very decent as a document drop.

I know you aren't commenting on hypothetical ideas - I was just elaborating on what I think about this object.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not saying there's zero potential for the topic. The magical-anorexia vibes are in the presentation. The the interview you're describing could very well be interesting, but I wasn't commenting on or criticizing hypothetical ideas.

As-is, the anon letter is the focal point of the article. It bugs me that it's 1) not original, when it would be very easy to write a similar letter, and 2) presumably/maybe written by a real person with an eating disorder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Um, how?
What makes it an SCP is that it inexplicably receives this sort of rants in hard copy.

Now what I'd like to see here is the foundation detaining an author of such a rant, and trying to interview him/her about why did they send anything, or whether they did at all. Depending on how well the interview was handled, this could be very brilliant, even using the above source.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… that pretty much seems to disqualify it from being an SCP, though. The Foundation can't really afford prolonged study of every odd cultural phenomena or fixation. Do some work? Yes. I just don't think eating disorders qualify… at least not as presented.

Because even if you blurred that line, it's still leaning too heavily on non-original content. SCPs like 173 certainly do some leaning, too, but there's a balance. Each needs the other for maximum effect.

…But I'll shut up about this one, now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nah.
The odd thing is that the mirror doesn't cause anorexia, in that case i'd downvote for reasons similar to when someone made Krokodil an SCP (people doing this sort of stuff to themselves from their own will is scarier than any sort of magic effect/disease/whatever) it is merely a recipient of a large volume of letters written by people with such.
I suppose it does feel slightly underdeveloped when you consider that the letter's contents are by and large irrelevant to why we contain it, and we'd do the same with a reindeer's horn that received pleas to Santa, but well, the execution is decent, and I still kind of like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Edited out my first, too-ranty comment.

This isn't so much offensive or shocking as …dumb. I'd be interested in hearing reasoning to the contrary, but right now this just reads like "hey what if anorexia was still anorexia BUT MADE BY WIZARDS" … even side-stepping words like "exploitative" (horror is the exploitation of our fears and uncertainties, yadda yadda), that just seems stupid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to agree with this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, if this is an actual letter, I firmly agree with this point.

All of the pathos and feeling comes from the writing of this uncredited person. They're given about the same credit as a freakshow centerpiece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … if this is a real letter, there's a pretty minimal amount of actual creative content, here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did you make the website?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do. Using someone else's art to make your writing more interesting is far, far less tasteless then using someone else's mental illness as the core of your article, especially one this uninspired.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hehe a mouse for the God of Gaming, a D20 for the Dice Gods.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just the tip of the iceberg. There is probably a shaving mirror for the god of muscle builders and a full length mirror for the plastic surgery-obsessed. All these people are psychiatrically ill.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't find him using a letter written by someone else any different than what's done with images.
Sure, it's that much neater when someone hacks together something on his own, see Mackenzie's Camera Disruption, but elsewhere taken images work very well, when you provide the fluff around them.
This is pretty much the same thing. And the fluff around it is well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea is maybe 5.5/10, but I really do love the writing style here. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I did above, and that's also why I added the link below. =/ Hmm, we didn't see any SCPs related to the topic though, so we figured we could give it a shot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, you should at least try to credit the actual author (maybe in the discussion?). And it's mostly the idea that deters me, it isn't clever or original to me at all. Letters to Ana are pretty common.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Which is why I'm considering rewriting a letter myself. But then that makes me think, which is more important in this site, whether or not the author is fully responsible for the article he/she has written, or simply whether or not the article is good (not that I'm saying this one definitely is). I'm quite on the fence over this right now.
